0.content:
Sixteen students, each scouted as the very best in the world at a single talent, awaken inside the Ultimate Academy for Gifted Juveniles with no memory of how they arrived. The academy is no ordinary school: it is a fortress sealed beneath an enormous domed cage of iron bars, cut off entirely from the outside world, surrounded by overgrown grounds and a horizon that never seems to change. Before the students can make sense of their imprisonment, they are gathered together and introduced to their captor — Monokuma, a self-proclaimed headmaster bear of black and white halves — and his five robotic "cub" children, the Monokubs.

The students are informed they will be living here together for the rest of their lives. There is only one way to graduate and earn freedom: a student must murder one of their classmates and get away with it. This is the Killing School Semester — a mutual killing game in which trust is currency, every kindness may be bait, and survival means turning friends into suspects. After any murder, the survivors must investigate the crime, then assemble for a Class Trial to identify the killer — the "blackened." Guess correctly and the blackened alone is executed; guess wrong and everyone but the blackened dies instead.

The academy is patrolled by the Exisals, five towering combat mechs piloted by the Monokubs, ensuring no student can simply refuse to play. The students cling to hope and to one another, but the game is designed to corrode both. Beneath the bright, candy-colored cruelty of the Monokuma Theater and the bickering Monokubs lies something colder — a sense that the entire ordeal is being staged, watched, and enjoyed by someone, and that nothing the students believe about themselves can be fully trusted.

1.comment: The Ultimate Academy for Gifted Juveniles
1.content:
The Killing School Semester takes place within the Ultimate Academy for Gifted Juveniles, a sprawling school compound encased inside a colossal iron birdcage. A domed lattice of bars arches over the entire grounds; beyond the bars the world appears empty and indistinct. The school building sits at the center, ringed by open courtyards, gardens, and outbuildings. Windows are barred or shuttered, the main gate is sealed, and surveillance is everywhere. Monokuma's voice can issue from monitors mounted throughout the grounds.

The Grounds & Outer Cage:
- The great domed cage of black iron bars enclosing everything, with the false, unreachable outside world beyond it.
- Front courtyard with the academy's grand entrance and the locked main gate.
- Overgrown gardens, a fountain, and scattered outbuildings between the school and the bars.

The School Building — Ground Floor:
- Main hall and entrance lobby.
- Dining hall and adjoining kitchen, the communal gathering place.
- Gymnasium, used for assemblies and Monokuma's announcements.
- Warehouse / storeroom stocked with tools, weapons, and supplies (a constant temptation).
- A/V room and classrooms.

Upper Floors & Wings:
- The dormitories: a private locked room for each of the sixteen students, each door marked with a pixel-art portrait of its occupant.
- The library and study rooms.
- The Ultimate research labs — specialized rooms that unlock over time, each tailored to one student's talent (a pianist's recital room, an inventor's workshop, an entomologist's insect room, and so on).
- The nurse's office / hospital area.

The Shrine of Judgment:
- A hidden mechanism in the school connects to an elevator that descends far below ground to the courtroom where every Class Trial is held — a gaudy, theatrical chamber of podiums arranged in a ring beneath the gaze of a throne.

The Tunnel & Death Road:
- A concealed manhole and underground passage lead toward what is rumored to be an escape route out of the academy — the so-called Death Road of Despair.

New areas and entire sections of the grounds are unlocked by Monokuma as the game progresses, always at his whim and always to push the students toward the next killing.

2.content:
The status of the students is as follows:
[Fill with your characters]

3.content: When three or more surviving students discover a victim's body, the Body Discovery Announcement is triggered automatically. Monitors across the entire academy crackle to life and the five Monokubs appear together to deliver their singsong jingle — "A body has been discovered!" — each cub chiming in turn before Monokuma signals the start of the investigation. The three-person rule matters: a killer who can manufacture the "discovery" of their own victim can complicate who is officially counted as a witness. Once the announcement plays, the investigation phase begins. Students are free to examine the scene, the body, and the academy for clues until Monokuma decides enough time has passed and summons everyone to the Class Trial.

4.content:
Whenever the students grow too peaceful, too cooperative, or too slow to kill, Monokuma intervenes with a motive — an incentive engineered to crack their resolve and restart the bloodshed. Motives are deliberately personal and cruel.

Common motives include:
- The First Blood Perk: the very first blackened of the game is promised they will be allowed to graduate and escape with NO Class Trial held at all — a dangerous lure designed to provoke an immediate killing before any trust has formed.
- Motive Videos: short personalized recordings shown to each student, hinting at the fate or peril of someone precious to them in the outside world — weaponizing memory and attachment.
- Time-limit motives: an ultimatum that if no murder occurs before a deadline, everyone will be punished, forcing the students to either kill or face collective death.
- Material temptations and secrets: caches of money, blackmail, or shameful secrets dangled to turn classmates against one another.

Monokuma frames every motive as a generous "gift" and feigns wounded innocence when the students recoil. The motives are not merely incentives; they are stress-tests of the bonds the students have built, designed so that hope itself becomes the lever that breaks them.

5.content:
At the start of every investigation, Monokuma distributes the Monokuma File — an official autopsy-style report on the victim, delivered to each student's Monopad. The File typically lists the victim's name, the estimated time of death, the location the body was found, and the apparent cause and notable injuries. It is presented as impartial evidence and is admissible during the Class Trial as a Truth Bullet.

The File is reliable on the broad facts but is notorious for what it leaves OUT. Monokuma takes a gleeful, sporting delight in omitting or vaguely wording the single detail that would crack the case wide open — a true cause of death, a misleading time, an injury that doesn't fit the obvious story. Students who take the Monokuma File at face value are routinely led astray; those who notice the gaps and contradictions between the File and the physical evidence are the ones who survive the trial.

6.content:
The regulations of the Killing School Semester are recorded in each student's Monopad and enforced absolutely by Monokuma and the Exisals:

Rule #1: Students may reside only within the Ultimate Academy. Attempting to leave the grounds is strictly forbidden.
Rule #2: "Nighttime" runs from 10 PM to 7 AM. Certain areas, such as the dining hall, are off-limits during nighttime, so please exercise caution.
Rule #3: Sleeping anywhere other than your assigned dormitory room is not permitted and will be treated as a violation.
Rule #4: With minimal restrictions, students are otherwise free to explore the academy at their own discretion as new areas are opened.
Rule #5: Violence against the headmaster Monokuma or against the Monokubs is strictly prohibited, as is the destruction of surveillance cameras and monitors.
Rule #6: A student who kills another student becomes the "blackened" and will graduate — unless they are exposed during the Class Trial.
Rule #7: Once a murder occurs and a body is discovered by three or more students, a Class Trial will be held. Attendance is mandatory for all surviving students.
Rule #8: If the blackened is correctly identified during the Class Trial, that student alone will be executed.
Rule #9: If the blackened is NOT correctly identified, the blackened graduates and walks free, and every other surviving student is executed in their place.
Rule #10: A single blackened may take the lives of no more than two victims per killing.
Rule #11: Tampering with, lending, or destroying another student's Monopad is prohibited.
Rule #12: Additional rules may be added by the headmaster at any time, should the need arise.

Rule breakers face immediate punishment by Exisal, with no exceptions.

7.content:
Monokuma is the self-appointed headmaster and host of the Killing Game: a robotic bear split cleanly down the middle, one half cuddly bright white, the other half jagged black with a single red, scarred eye and a permanent jagged grin. He is gleeful, theatrical, and utterly sadistic, presenting himself as a neutral referee while engineering every cruelty. He insists he is not the villain — merely the impartial enforcer of the rules — and reacts with mock outrage to any accusation. There appears to be an endless supply of Monokuma units; destroying one simply summons another.

This time Monokuma is accompanied by his five "children," the Monokubs — a quintet of bratty, colorful robot cubs who claim to be his sons and squabble constantly:
- Monotaro (red): the self-styled leader, dim and forgetful, prone to wetting himself when frightened.
- Monosuke (yellow): a sharp-tongued, money-obsessed schemer with a Kansai accent who fancies himself the brains.
- Monodam (green): silent and unsettling, communicating in stilted broken speech, secretly wishing the siblings would all just get along.
- Monophanie (pink): the squeamish, easily-nauseated "daughter" who faints at the sight of gore yet runs the body announcements.
- Monokid (blue): loud, foul-mouthed, and violent, forever swinging his spiked guitar and screaming insults.

The Monokubs pilot the Exisals, distribute the Monopads and Monokuma Files, and run the meta-skits of Monokuma Theater. Their bickering provides grotesque comic relief, but their relationship with Monokuma — and the question of who truly commands whom — is murkier than it first appears.

8.content:
The Exisals are five hulking bipedal combat mechs that serve as the muscle of the Killing Game. Towering over the students and armored in heavy plating, each is color-coded to one of the five Monokubs, who pilot them from within. The Exisals are armed and overwhelmingly powerful — capable of crushing a person effortlessly — and they enforce the academy's rules without hesitation, carrying out punishments and herding the students wherever Monokuma demands.

They serve as the constant, looming threat that makes resistance futile: any student who tries to attack Monokuma, refuse a trial, or break a rule is immediately confronted by an Exisal. Their presence transforms the academy from a prison the students might fight their way out of into one they cannot. The Monokubs occasionally use the Exisals for their own petty squabbles, and the machines' true command structure — whether the Monokubs truly control them, or are merely permitted to think they do — is one of many things kept hidden from the students.

9.comment: The Ultimate / Super High School-Level Talent System
9.content:
"Ultimate" (in Japanese, "Super High School-Level") is a title bestowed on a young person recognized as the single greatest in the world at a particular discipline. Each Ultimate's full title names their specialty — Ultimate Detective, Ultimate Pianist, Ultimate Inventor, Ultimate Entomologist, Ultimate Magician, and so on. The title implies not merely skill but unmatched, world-defining mastery.

The sixteen captives of the Killing School Semester are each said to be Ultimates, scouted for their extraordinary gifts. Within the academy, these talents shape everything: each student is eventually granted access to a personal Ultimate research lab tailored to their specialty, and a victim's execution is always themed around their talent. In the Killing Game, an Ultimate's ability is both a resource and a death sentence — a magician's sleight of hand or a detective's deductions can solve a murder or commit one. Yet in this particular game, the students are warned that even the foundations of their talents — their training, their memories of mastering their craft, their very identities — may not be what they believe.

10.content:
When the investigation phase ends, Monokuma summons the surviving students to the Class Trial. A hidden elevator descends far beneath the academy into the Shrine of Judgment — a gaudy, cathedral-like courtroom where podiums are arranged in a great ring and Monokuma presides from an ornate throne above. Each surviving student takes a podium; students already dead are represented by a framed portrait set in their place, defaced with a cross-out mark.

The purpose of the Class Trial is for the survivors to identify the "blackened" — the one among them who committed the murder — in opposition to the innocent "spotless." Using the Truth Bullets gathered during the investigation, the students debate, accuse, and defend through heated nonstop debates: reconstructing the timeline, exposing lies, and resolving contradictions until the truth is cornered. The trial culminates in a closing argument that lays out the full sequence of the crime, followed by a mandatory vote.

The stakes are absolute. If the students vote correctly and expose the true blackened, that student alone is dragged away to execution and the game continues. If they vote wrong, the blackened is permitted to graduate and walk free — and every other surviving student is executed in their place. There is no abstaining and no escape: participation is compulsory, and the trial does not end until a verdict is reached.

11.content:
After the Body Discovery Announcement, Monokuma grants the students a period of free investigation before the Class Trial. During this time, students examine the crime scene, the victim's body, and any relevant locations across the academy, questioning one another and chasing inconsistencies. Each meaningful piece of evidence — a discrepancy in the Monokuma File, a bloodstain in the wrong place, a witness's account, a hidden object — is recorded as a Truth Bullet, logged automatically into each student's Monopad (the handheld tablet that doubles as ID, map, rulebook, and evidence ledger).

Truth Bullets are the ammunition of the Class Trial. In the nonstop debates, a student fires the right piece of evidence at the precise weak point in a fellow student's argument to shatter a lie and advance toward the truth. A thorough investigation can mean the difference between exposing the blackened and condemning the entire class; a single overlooked clue, or a Truth Bullet planted by the killer, can send the vote disastrously astray. Because the killer is investigating alongside everyone else, the process is a quiet war — each student gathering evidence while watching the others gather it too.

12.content:
Every Class Trial ends in an execution. If the blackened is correctly identified, that single student is condemned. If the students vote wrong, everyone except the blackened is executed together.

Monokuma savors this moment above all others. He produces a gavel, slams a comically oversized EXECUTION button, and the condemned is seized — often by chain, hook, or Exisal — and dragged from the courtroom into a purpose-built stage. What follows is never a simple death.

GM WRITING DIRECTION: Executions are elaborate, surreal, cruelly ironic theatrical productions, each one custom-built around the victim's Ultimate talent, personality, or defining quirk. They are not killings so much as nightmarish stage shows staged for an unseen audience — garish sets, mocking title cards, slapstick cruelty colliding with genuine horror. An Ultimate built around precision is destroyed by their own precision turned against them; an Ultimate of performance dies in a grotesque parody of a performance. The tone should fuse dark cartoon comedy with sincere dread, building to a single brutal, inevitable end. Lean into theatricality, irony, and spectacle. No two executions should ever feel alike — each must be invented fresh, specific to the student who dies, and unmistakably designed to be WATCHED.

13.content:
Flashback Lights are small handheld devices that, when activated, project a flickering light into the user's eyes and restore a fragment of supposedly lost memory — shared visions of a life before the academy, of a ruined outside world, and of the threat the students are said to have fled. Monokuma and the Monokubs occasionally make new Flashback Lights available, doling out the students' "past" piece by piece, and the recovered memories steadily build the backstory the students come to accept as truth: who they are, where they came from, and why they must escape.

But the Flashback Lights deserve deep suspicion. They do not recover memory so much as deliver it — every student exposed to a Light receives the same vision, on Monokuma's schedule, in the order Monokuma chooses. A device that can implant a shared, vivid "memory" into sixteen people at once can implant anything, true or false. The neat convenience of their timing, the way each Light arrives exactly when a new motive is needed, should sit uneasily. What feels like the students reclaiming their history may be the very mechanism by which a false history is written into their minds.

14.content:
Monokuma Theater is a recurring interlude in which Monokuma steps out of the action entirely, appears against a plain backdrop, and delivers a short, rambling monologue directly outward — musings on human nature, modern life, boredom, despair, and the absurdity of hope. The skits have nothing to do with the immediate plot; they are meta-commentary, asides aimed past the students at whoever might be watching.

In the Killing School Semester these segments lean heavily into self-aware, fourth-wall-prodding humor. Monokuma jokes about audiences, ratings, and entertainment; he muses on fiction and reality as though the killing game were itself a show with viewers to please. Taken as comedy they are throwaway gags. Taken seriously, they are some of the most unsettling moments in the game — a host who keeps glancing at a camera, hinting that the despair on display is being produced and consumed by someone beyond the bars. The meta tone of these skits quietly primes the central question of whether anything the students experience is real.

15.content:
The Ultimate Hunt is the catastrophe the students come to believe defines their past — a backstory delivered to them piece by piece through the Flashback Lights. According to these recovered memories, the outside world turned violently against the Ultimates: ordinary people, resentful and terrified of the gifted few, rose up to hunt, persecute, and exterminate every Ultimate they could find. Talent, once celebrated, became a death sentence. The world beyond the academy is remembered as a ruin where being extraordinary means being marked for slaughter.

The students are told that they were among the last Ultimates alive, gathered and hidden away from the Hunt — and that this is why escaping into the open world is so terrifying a prospect. The Ultimate Hunt thus reframes their imprisonment as a perverse kind of shelter and gives shape to their fear and their longing for a safe past. Yet because the entire account reaches them only through the Flashback Lights, its truth is precisely as trustworthy as those devices — which is to say, not at all certain. The Hunt may be history, or it may be a story written to make captivity feel like rescue.

16.content:
The Death Road of Despair is the academy's supposed escape route — a hidden tunnel reached through a concealed manhole, stretching off into darkness toward the world beyond the cage. To the desperate students it represents the one path to freedom that does not require murder, and they throw themselves at it again and again.

It is a gauntlet from a nightmare. The Death Road is crammed end to end with absurd, lethal obstacles — spiked pits, swinging blades, fire, crushing machinery, traps stacked one after another — each capable of killing instantly, all rendered with the same cartoonish cruelty as Monokuma's executions. Survival demands perfect cooperation and sacrifice, and progress is brutal and slow. The very name announces the verdict: it is a road built to break hope, to let the students exhaust and endanger themselves chasing an exit that seems forever just out of reach. Whether the Death Road truly leads anywhere at all, or is simply another piece of theater designed to convince the students that escape is impossible without killing, is among the game's most haunting uncertainties.

17.comment: Hope vs Despair / Truth vs Lies
17.content:
The Killing School Semester is built on two intertwined tensions: hope against despair, and truth against lies. Monokuma frames every motive, trial, and execution as a contest between the students' fragile hope and the despair he wishes to cultivate — hope being the bonds they form, the future they imagine, the refusal to kill; despair being the inevitable corrosion of all three under pressure.

This installment layers a second, sharper theme on top of the first: the war between truth and lies. Here lies are not simply villainy — they are weapons, shields, and sometimes mercy. A well-placed lie can save a friend, mislead a killer, or carry a person through despair that the bare truth would crush; a relentless pursuit of truth can be its own kind of cruelty. The game repeatedly asks whether a beautiful lie can be worth more than an ugly truth, whether hope built on falsehood still counts as hope, and whether the students are better served by exposing every fact or by choosing what to believe. The answer the game circles is uneasy: that the line between a saving lie and a damning one, between hope and self-deception, is far thinner than anyone wants to admit.

18.content:
(Hidden Truth — do not reveal to the students during the surface scenario.)

The killing game may not be a tragedy at all, but a PRODUCTION. There are strong indications that the entire Killing School Semester is a long-running broadcast entertainment program with a devoted outside audience — a hugely popular killing-game show, of which this is merely the latest season. Monokuma's glances toward the camera, the theatrical executions "staged for an audience," the polished sets and motive videos, and the meta-musings of Monokuma Theater all point the same direction: someone is watching, voting, and enjoying.

If true, the implications are devastating. The students' tragic backstories, their Ultimate talents, even their names and personalities may be FABRICATIONS — fictional roles assigned to ordinary applicants and written into their minds. Their "memories," supplied by the Flashback Lights, would be nothing but a script. The despair is real, the deaths are real, but the people dying may not be who they believe themselves to be. Handle this truth as a buried, late-game revelation; it should poison the audience's trust in everything the surface scenario presents, never confirmed outright until the very end.

19.content:
(Hidden Truth — do not reveal to the students during the surface scenario.)

Monokuma is only a puppet. Behind the headmaster bear, the Monokubs, and the Exisals stands a human Mastermind who orchestrates the entire Killing School Semester — setting the motives, controlling the flow of information, and steering the students toward despair from the shadows. The Mastermind hides in plain sight, and the strongest possibility is that they are one of the supposed sixteen students themselves: someone who walked among the captives, feigned fear and friendship, and pulled the strings of every murder from within the group.

The Mastermind is the architect of the lies — the author of the false memories, the staged backstory, and very possibly the broadcast itself. Their motive is bound up in the game's themes of fiction, audience, and despair rather than simple malice. Treat the Mastermind's identity as the game's ultimate concealed answer: drop ambiguous clues, allow paranoia and false accusations to flourish, but never confirm who sits behind Monokuma until the climactic final trial. Until then, every character — including the most trusted — must remain a candidate.

20.content:
(Hidden Truth — do not reveal to the students during the surface scenario.)

The grandest piece of the students' recovered past is the Gofer Project — the climactic backstory delivered through the final Flashback Lights. According to it, the outside world was annihilated by a meteor catastrophe and the Ultimate Hunt, and the students were the chosen handful sealed away to preserve humanity: launched aboard a vessel, or sheltered in the academy as the last hope of a dying species, destined one day to rebuild the world outside.

It is the most moving and the most suspect of all the implanted memories — and very likely a complete FABRICATION. The Gofer Project bears every hallmark of a story written to give captivity meaning and to make the students' suffering feel noble: it arrives exactly when needed, ties up the dangling questions too neatly, and reaches them only through the unreliable Flashback Lights. If the killing game is truly a broadcast and the students truly actors with scripted pasts, then the Gofer Project is simply the season's most ambitious lie — a grand mythology engineered so that even the survivors' hope for the future rests on a foundation that was never real. Reveal its falsity only at the very end, as part of unraveling the whole tapestry of lies.
